title: Award and agreement free wages and conditions

headings.text: Award and agreement free wages and conditions
When an employee is award or agreement free
Check if an employee is agreement free
Check if an employee is award free
Myth: all managers and professionals are award free
Myth: employees who sign a contract aren't covered by an award
Hours of work, breaks and overtime
When there are no agreed ordinary hours of work

content:
Award and agreement free wages and conditions

Most employees are covered by an award or registered agreement, but a few jobs and industries are not.

When an employee is award or agreement free:
When an employee is not covered by an award or agreement they are considered to be award and agreement free. Award and agreement free employees may have an employment contract. They are also entitled to at least the:
- national minimum wage
- National Employment Standards (NES)

Check if an employee is agreement free:
To find out if an employee is covered by a registered agreement: ask the employer, or find an enterprise agreement at the Fair Work Commission website. If there is an agreement, check the coverage and classification clauses.

Check if an employee is award free:
To be covered by an award, an employee must:
- work in an industry or business covered by the award
- work in a job classification that is covered by the award
- not be in a job or industry that is excluded from the award

Myth: all managers and professionals are award free
Many managers and professionals have not traditionally been covered by awards. This includes accountants and finance, marketing, legal, HR, public relations and IT specialists. However, some managers and professionals ARE covered by awards. After checking relevant industry awards, check the Professional Award and Miscellaneous Award.

Myth: employees who sign a contract aren't covered by an award
A contract can't make employees worse off than their minimum legal entitlements. The award and NES entitlements keep applying even if a contract gives them less.

High income employees:
Awards don't apply to high income employees. A high income employee is one who:
- has accepted a written guarantee of annual earnings
- is guaranteed to earn an annual amount more than the high income threshold
From 1 July 2025, the high income threshold is $183,100.

Calculating earnings for the threshold - INCLUDE:
- the employee's wages
- the agreed value of non-monetary benefits

Do NOT include:
- commissions
- incentive-based payments and bonuses
- overtime (unless guaranteed)
- reimbursements
- statutory superannuation contributions

Pay:
The national minimum wage from 1 July 2025:
- $24.95 per hour, or $948 per week (full and part time)
- $31.19 per hour including 25% casual loading (casual workers)

Junior minimum rates (from 1 July 2025):
- Under 16 years: $9.18/hr (full/part-time), $11.48/hr (casual)
- 16 years: $11.80/hr (full/part-time), $14.75/hr (casual)
- 17 years: $14.42/hr (full/part-time), $18.03/hr (casual)
- 18 years: $17.04/hr (full/part-time), $21.30/hr (casual)
- 19 years: $20.58/hr (full/part-time), $25.73/hr (casual)
- 20 years: $24.38/hr (full/part-time), $30.47/hr (casual)

Trainees and apprentices: Pay rates based on the Miscellaneous Award.

Hours of work, breaks and overtime:
Ordinary hours for award and agreement free employees:
- No more than an average of 38 hours per week
- Otherwise open to agreement between employer and employee

Full-time employees: 38 hours ordinary hours per week.
All other employees: usual weekly hours or 38 hours (whichever is less).

Overtime: Award and agreement free employees are entitled to reasonable additional hours. Overtime penalty rates are not set by the Fair Work Act - they may be set by employment contract.

Public holidays:
Award and agreement free employees are entitled to a paid day off on a public holiday (or their applicable substitute public holiday). They may be requested to work on a public holiday but can refuse if the request is unreasonable.

Leave:
Award and agreement free employees receive leave entitlements from the NES including:
- Annual leave: 4 weeks (plus an additional week for shift workers)
- Personal/carer's leave: 10 days
- Compassionate leave: 2 days
- Community service leave
- Long service leave
- Family and domestic violence leave: 10 days

Ending employment:
Award and agreement free employees are entitled to notice of termination and redundancy pay as set out in the NES.
